refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall provide an integrated performance requirement derivation, recording, and management system that translates worksite mission context (site conditions, operational policies, safety constraints) into documented performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) with full traceability to source inputs and maintained history of baseline calibration updates.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit connection to worksite inputs and traceability mechanisms. Refinement clarifies that derivation is a transformation process from mission context to measurable targets, and that recording/management includes both forward traceability (target ← input) and historical baseline tracking. This addresses the acceptance criterion requiring documented traceability and calibration history.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (1) performance targets are derived from documented worksite inputs via a defined methodology; (2) each target has a recorded source reference; (3) baseline versions and calibration updates are timestamped and retrievable; (4) traceability is bidirectional (input→target and target→input).
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The system shall capture and store worksite mission context (site conditions, operational policies, safety constraints) as structured input data.
CR-NGHE-005.2: The system shall apply a documented derivation methodology to transform worksite mission context into performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability).
CR-NGHE-005.3: The system shall maintain forward and reverse traceability links between worksite inputs and derived performance targets.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The system shall record and version-control baseline performance targets and calibration updates with timestamps and change rationale.
CR-NGHE-005.5: The system shall provide query and reporting capability to retrieve performance targets and their derivation history for audit and compliance purposes.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized performance management database with embedded derivation rules engine, traceability metadata schema, and versioning control; integrated with worksite input capture module and reporting interface.
DP-B: Distributed performance target calculation across vehicle telemetry processing and teleoperation command planning, with eventual consistency synchronization to a central audit log.
DP-C: Manual performance target specification by Site Manager via structured form interface, with automated traceability recording and baseline snapshot functionality.

DP-D: Hybrid approach: vehicle and teleoperation modules compute operational performance metrics in real-time; Safety Services module aggregates and validates against policy constraints; centralized repository maintains traceability and history.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid approach with distributed computation (Vehicle and Teleoperation modules generate operational performance metrics), Safety Services validation (policy constraint checking), and centralized Performance Management Repository (traceability, versioning, audit trail). This design separates concerns: operational metrics are computed where data is generated; safety/policy validation is centralized in Safety Services; historical record-keeping and traceability are isolated in a dedicated repository.

potential_sources: Coupling between Vehicle/Teleoperation metric computation and Safety Services validation policy definitions (mitigated by well-defined interface contract for performance metric schema and policy rule format).
Coupling between Safety Services and Performance Repository on baseline update triggers (mitigated by event-driven interface with clear ownership of baseline approval authority).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits low coupling because computation, validation, and storage are functionally separated. The primary risk is semantic coupling on performance metric definitions (e.g., 'cycle time' definition must be consistent across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services); this is managed via a shared data dictionary and interface specification.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context can be captured in structured form (not free-text narrative only).
ASSUMPTION: Performance target derivation rules are deterministic and can be codified (not purely expert judgment).
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can emit performance metrics with sufficient fidelity and timeliness for real-time validation.
RISK: If derivation rules are complex or frequently change, the hybrid approach may require frequent updates across multiple modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).
RISK: Distributed metric computation may introduce inconsistency if Vehicle and Teleoperation use different calculation methods for the same metric (e.g., cycle time).
RISK: High safety criticality requires that performance targets derived from safety constraints are validated before use; Safety Services module must have authority to reject or flag targets.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify ownership of performance metric definitions and derivation rule authority. Recommend that Safety Services owns the policy constraint rules and has veto authority over performance targets that violate safety policies. Vehicle and Teleoperation modules should compute operational metrics but not interpret policy compliance; that interpretation is Safety Services' responsibility. This reduces coupling and clarifies accountability.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Safety Services module is assigned ownership of the performance requirement derivation, recording, and management system. This module shall: (1) define and maintain performance target derivation rules and policy constraints; (2) receive operational performance metrics from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via Telemetry interface; (3) validate derived targets against safety and operational policies; (4) maintain the authoritative performance target repository with full traceability and versioning; (5) expose performance targets and history via SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces for consumption by other modules and external stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services receives operational performance metrics (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability measurements) from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. Telemetry carries raw or aggregated performance data that feeds the derivation and validation logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services receives worksite mission context (site conditions, operational policies, safety constraints) via Tasking interface from Site Manager or external mission planning system. This context is the input to the derivation methodology.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services emits performance target updates, baseline calibration notifications, and policy compliance alerts via SafetyAlert interface to Equipment Operator, Tele-operator, and Maintenance/Quality stakeholders.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services uses Network Link for synchronization with remote audit/compliance systems and for distributed query access to performance target history and traceability records.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own performance management logic. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all sub-modules and obscure ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle module computes operational metrics (e.g., cycle time from sensor data) but should not own policy validation or historical record-keeping. Performance management is a cross-cutting concern that spans Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Assigning to Vehicle alone would create diffusion of traceability and policy logic. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module computes remote-operation performance metrics but, like Vehicle, should not own policy validation or historical management. Assigning to Teleoperation would create asymmetry with Vehicle-side metrics and diffuse policy authority. Not recommended.

Safety Services is the natural owner of performance requirement management because: (1) it is responsible for policy constraint validation (high safety criticality); (2) it has authority to approve/reject performance targets based on safety rules; (3) it can centralize traceability and versioning without diffusion; (4) it interfaces with Tasking (mission context input) and SafetyAlert (stakeholder notifications); (5) it can aggregate metrics from Vehicle and Teleoperation via Telemetry without duplicating logic. Recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary because performance requirement management is fundamentally a safety and policy governance function. The module receives mission context (Tasking), validates operational metrics against policy constraints, and maintains the authoritative record of approved performance targets. This centralizes traceability and policy authority while allowing Vehicle and Teleoperation to focus on metric computation. The high safety criticality of CR-NGHE-005 aligns with Safety Services' core responsibility.
containment_risks: RISK: Safety Services may become a bottleneck if performance target derivation and validation are synchronous and high-latency. Mitigation: implement asynchronous validation with caching of approved targets for real-time use.

RISK: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules compute metrics independently without coordination, Safety Services may receive inconsistent or conflicting performance data. Mitigation: establish a shared metric definition schema and require Vehicle/Teleoperation to conform to it.

RISK: Worksite mission context (Tasking input) may be incomplete or ambiguous, making derivation rules difficult to apply. Mitigation: define a structured worksite input schema and require Site Manager to provide complete context before performance targets are derived.
RISK: Historical traceability records may grow large, impacting query performance. Mitigation: implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) and archive old baselines to a separate audit repository.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Safety Services has the capacity and authority to own performance requirement management. If Safety Services is already heavily loaded with other safety-critical functions, consider creating a dedicated Performance Management sub-module within Safety Services to isolate this concern. Ensure that the Tasking interface contract clearly specifies the structure and completeness requirements for worksite mission context input.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid distributed computation with centralized Safety Services validation and repository) directly addresses all sub-FRs: (1) worksite input capture is via Tasking interface to Safety Services; (2) derivation methodology is implemented in Safety Services rules engine; (3) traceability is maintained in Safety Services repository; (4) baseline versioning and calibration history are managed by Safety Services; (5) query/reporting is exposed via SafetyAlert and Network Link interfaces. The DP is complete and non-redundant.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): Vehicle and Teleoperation modules compute metrics independently; if not coordinated, they may produce inconsistent results. Mitigation: enforce shared metric definition schema via interface contract.

DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): Worksite mission context (Tasking) may be interpreted differently by Safety Services and other modules. Mitigation: centralize interpretation logic in Safety Services; other modules consume only the derived performance targets, not the raw context.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is a high-containment, low-diffusion choice. All traceability, versioning, and policy validation logic is centralized. Vehicle and Teleoperation are responsible only for metric computation, not interpretation or governance. This clear separation of concerns minimizes diffusion and maximizes containment.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) worksite input capture can be verified by inspecting Tasking interface data and Safety Services input schema; (2) derivation methodology can be verified by tracing sample inputs through the rules engine and comparing outputs to expected targets; (3) traceability can be verified by querying the repository for forward/reverse links; (4) baseline versioning can be verified by inspecting version history and timestamps; (5) query/reporting can be verified by executing sample queries and validating results.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services owns CR-NGHE-005 and all sub-FRs. Vehicle and Teleoperation own the sub-FRs for metric computation (to be decomposed in subsequent levels). Site Manager and Maintenance/Quality are consumers of the performance targets and history, not owners.

recommended_revisions: Establish a formal interface contract for Tasking input to Safety Services, specifying the required structure and completeness of worksite mission context.
Define a shared performance metric schema (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services all conform to.
Clarify the approval workflow for performance targets: who has authority to approve/reject targets, and what is the escalation path if Safety Services flags a target as non-compliant?
Specify the retention policy for baseline versions and calibration history (e.g., keep last 12 months, archive older records).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 is not atomic; it has been refined into five sub-FRs (input capture, derivation methodology, traceability, versioning, query/reporting). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to identify specific DPs and IMs. The FR-DP-IM mapping at this level is sound (DP-D is well-coupled to the refined FR, and Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary), but the sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level to identify concrete implementation modules and interfaces. Proceeding to the next level will clarify how worksite input capture is realized, how derivation rules are encoded, how traceability is stored and queried, and how versioning is managed.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The system shall capture and store worksite mission context (site conditions, operational policies, safety constraints) as structured input data.
CR-NGHE-005.2: The system shall apply a documented derivation methodology to transform worksite mission context into performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability).
CR-NGHE-005.3: The system shall maintain forward and reverse traceability links between worksite inputs and derived performance targets.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The system shall record and version-control baseline performance targets and calibration updates with timestamps and change rationale.
CR-NGHE-005.5: The system shall provide query and reporting capability to retrieve performance targets and their derivation history for audit and compliance purposes.

assumptions: Worksite mission context can be captured in structured form (not free-text narrative only).
Performance target derivation rules are deterministic and can be codified.
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can emit performance metrics with sufficient fidelity and timeliness.
Safety Services has the capacity and authority to own performance requirement management.
Tasking interface can reliably deliver worksite mission context to Safety Services.
SafetyAlert interface can reliably deliver performance target updates to stakeholders.
open_risks: RISK: Distributed metric computation (Vehicle, Teleoperation) may introduce inconsistency if calculation methods differ. Mitigation: enforce shared metric definition schema.
RISK: Safety Services may become a bottleneck if validation is synchronous and high-latency. Mitigation: implement asynchronous validation with caching.
RISK: Worksite mission context may be incomplete or ambiguous. Mitigation: define structured input schema and require complete context.
RISK: Historical traceability records may grow large, impacting query performance. Mitigation: implement tiered storage and archiving.
RISK: High safety criticality requires that performance targets are validated before use; Safety Services must have veto authority. Mitigation: establish formal approval workflow.
evidence_needed: Worksite mission context schema (structure, required fields, data types).
Performance metric definitions (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) with calculation formulas.
Derivation rules engine specification (rule syntax, evaluation logic, conflict resolution).
Traceability data model (forward/reverse link representation, query language).
Versioning and baseline management specification (version numbering, retention policy, change log format).
Query and reporting interface specification (query types, output formats, access control).
Safety Services capacity assessment (current load, available resources for performance management).
Tasking and SafetyAlert interface contracts (data schemas, timing requirements, reliability guarantees).
